s0-g0.label: Calculus
s0-g0.desc: Calculus is a branch of mathematics that deals with continuous change. It provides tools and techniques for understanding rates of change and accumulation. The two main branches are differential calculus, which focuses on finding the rate of change of a function, and integral calculus, which focuses on finding the accumulation of quantities. These concepts are fundamental for optimization, modeling, and understanding the behavior of functions.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: A **derivative** measures how fast something changes — the slope of a curve at a point. In machine learning, training a model means *minimising a loss function*, and the derivative tells you which direction reduces the error.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: With many inputs you use **partial derivatives**, collected into a **gradient** vector that points uphill (so you step the opposite way). The **chain rule** lets you differentiate functions composed of other functions — exactly what a neural network is. Second-order info lives in the **Hessian**; the **Jacobian** generalises gradients to vector outputs.

levels.label: In ML
levels.body: Gradient descent is the workhorse: `θ ← θ − η·∇L(θ)`, where `η` is the learning rate. **Back-propagation** is just the chain rule applied efficiently over the network's computation graph, reusing intermediate results from the forward pass.

s0-g1.label: Linear Algebra
s0-g1.desc: Linear algebra is a branch of mathematics that deals with vector spaces and linear transformations between those spaces. It involves concepts like vectors, matrices, and systems of linear equations, and provides tools for manipulating and solving problems involving these entities. Operations such as matrix multiplication, decomposition, and eigenvalue analysis are fundamental to this field.

links.title: How I learned Linear Algebra, Probability and Statistics for Data Science

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Data lives as **vectors** (a row of numbers), a dataset is a **matrix**, and batches of images or sequences are **tensors** (n-dimensional arrays). Getting comfortable thinking in these shapes is half of practical ML.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Matrix multiplication** applies a linear transformation; a **dot product** measures similarity/alignment between vectors. Know **transpose**, **inverse**, and **rank**. **Eigenvalues/eigenvectors** and the **SVD** reveal the directions a transformation stretches or compresses — the basis for dimensionality reduction.

levels.label: In ML
levels.body: A dense layer is literally `y = Wx + b`. Word/image **embeddings** are learned vectors where distance encodes meaning. **PCA** is an eigen-decomposition (or SVD) of the data's covariance, keeping the directions of greatest variance.

s0-g2.label: Discrete Mathematics
s0-g2.desc: Discrete mathematics deals with mathematical structures that are fundamentally discrete rather than continuous. This means it focuses on objects that have distinct, separated values, like integers, graphs, and logical statements. It provides the theoretical foundations and tools for reasoning about and modeling these discrete structures. This field is essential for computer science, as it provides the foundation for understanding algorithms, data structures, and information networks.

links.title: Discrete Math (Full Course: Sets, Logic, Proofs, Probability, Graph Theory, etc)

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: The math of distinct structures: **logic**, **sets**, **combinatorics** (counting), and **graphs**. Less about smooth change, more about how things are arranged and connected.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Graph theory** (nodes, edges, paths, trees), **counting** (permutations and combinations), and **proof by induction**. Big-O analysis of algorithms lives here too.

levels.label: In ML
levels.body: **Graphs** model computation (every neural net is a directed graph) and relationships (knowledge graphs, GNNs). **Combinatorics** underpins probability counts, and **Boolean logic** is the language of decision trees and rule systems.

s0-g3.label: Statistics
s0-g3.desc: Statistics is the science of collecting, analyzing, interpreting, presenting, and organizing data. It is a branch of mathematics that deals with the collection, analysis, interpretation, presentation, and organization of data. It is used in a wide range of fields, including science, engineering, medicine, and social science. Statistics is used to make informed decisions, to predict future events, and to test hypotheses. It is also used to summarize data, to describe relationships between variables, and to make inferences about populations based on samples.

links.title: Statistics - A Full University Course on Data Science Basics

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Probability** quantifies uncertainty; a **random variable** takes values with given likelihoods; a **distribution** describes those likelihoods. Summarise data with **mean**, **variance**, and simple charts before modelling anything.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Bayes' theorem** updates a belief given new evidence: `P(A|B) ∝ P(B|A)·P(A)`. Know the common **distributions** (normal, Bernoulli, binomial, Poisson), **sampling**, and **inferential statistics** — estimating parameters, hypothesis tests, and confidence intervals.

levels.label: In ML
levels.body: Most loss functions are **negative log-likelihoods** under an assumed distribution. **Naïve Bayes** applies Bayes directly; significance testing tells you whether a metric gain is real; and modelling uncertainty (not just point predictions) is what separates good ML from naïve ML.

s1-g0.label: Python
s1-g0.desc: Python is an interpreted high-level general-purpose programming language. Its design philosophy emphasizes code readability with its significant use of indentation. Its language constructs as well as its object-oriented approach aim to help programmers write clear, logical code for small and large-scale projects. Python is dynamically-typed and garbage-collected. It supports multiple programming paradigms, including structured (particularly, procedural), object-oriented and functional programming. Python is often described as a "batteries included" language due to its comprehensive standard library.

links.title: Visit the Dedicated Python Developer Roadmap
Tutorial Series: How to Code in Python

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: A readable, high-level language that became the **lingua franca of ML** because of its ecosystem. If you know another language, you'll move fast — focus on idioms, not syntax.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Get fluent with the **standard library**, **comprehensions**, **generators**, and **virtual environments**. Then the data trio: **NumPy** arrays, **pandas** frames, **Matplotlib** plots.

levels.label: In ML
levels.body: The real skill is the stack: **NumPy** (math), **pandas** (data), **scikit-learn** (classical ML), and **PyTorch/TensorFlow** (deep learning). Write *vectorised* code (operate on whole arrays) instead of Python loops — it's often 10–100× faster.

s1-g1.label: Basic Syntax
s1-g1.desc: Python's basic syntax defines the rules for writing code that the interpreter can understand and execute. This includes how to structure lines of code, use indentation to define code blocks, write comments for explanation, assign values to variables, and perform basic operations using operators. Understanding these fundamental elements is essential for writing any Python program, including those used in machine learning.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Variables and **data types**, **control flow** (`if`, `for`, `while`), **functions**, **exceptions**, and the core **data structures**: list, dict, set, tuple. This is the 20% you use 80% of the time.

levels.label: Handy idioms
levels.body: List/dict **comprehensions** (`[f(x) for x in xs]`), **slicing** (`a[1:5]`), **f-strings** (`f"{x:.2f}"`), unpacking, and generators. These make data-wrangling code short and clear.

levels.label: In ML
levels.body: Most ML code is **NumPy/pandas** calls glued together with comprehensions and functions. Write **vectorised** operations over arrays instead of Python loops — often 10–100× faster.

s1-g2.label: Object Oriented Programming
s1-g2.desc: In Python, object-oriented Programming (OOPs) is a programming paradigm that uses objects and classes in programming. It aims to implement real-world entities like inheritance, polymorphism, encapsulation, etc., in programming. The main concept of OOPs is to bind the data and the functions that work on that together as a single unit so that no other part of the code can access this data.

links.title: Object Oriented Programming with Python - Full Course for Beginners
Object Oriented Programming (OOP) In Python - Beginner Crash Course

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Bundle data and behaviour into **classes** that create **objects**. Key ideas: **encapsulation** (hide internals), **inheritance** (reuse/extend), and **polymorphism** (same interface, different implementations).

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: In Python everything is an **object**. Use `__init__` for setup, **dunder methods** (`__len__`, `__call__`) for built-in behaviour, and prefer **composition** over deep inheritance.

levels.label: In ML
levels.body: Frameworks are object-oriented: a PyTorch model subclasses `nn.Module`, datasets subclass `Dataset`, transforms are composable objects. Understanding OOP lets you read and extend these instead of only calling them.

s1-g3.label: Essential libraries
s1-g3.desc: Python's popularity in data analysis stems from its rich ecosystem of specialized libraries. **NumPy** provides powerful tools for numerical computation, particularly with arrays and matrices. **Pandas** offers data structures like DataFrames, making it easy to organize, manipulate, and analyze tabular data. For visualization, **Matplotlib** is a fundamental library for creating static, interactive, and animated plots. Building on Matplotlib, **Seaborn** provides a high-level interface for drawing attractive and informative statistical graphics. These libraries collectively enable efficient data handling, exploration, and presentation.

levels.label: NumPy
levels.body: `ndarray` is the core: fast, typed, n-dimensional arrays. Learn **vectorisation** and **broadcasting** (operating on arrays of different shapes without explicit loops).

levels.label: pandas
levels.body: `DataFrame`/`Series` for tabular data — loading, cleaning, filtering, grouping, and joining. Most real ML time is spent here, preparing data.

levels.label: Matplotlib / Seaborn
levels.body: Plot distributions and relationships to *understand* your data before modelling. Seaborn gives you statistical charts (histograms, pairplots, heatmaps) in one line.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Beyond any single language: how programs actually run, core **data structures & algorithms**, **version control** (git), and comfort on the **command line**. These transfer to every ML tool you'll touch.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Know the **complexity** of common operations (list vs set lookup), use **git branches** for experiments, and get comfortable in the **terminal** — most ML tooling is command-line first.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: For ML specifically: reading/writing data through **APIs and databases**, structuring **reproducible projects**, isolating **environments** (`venv`/`conda`), and knowing when to use a quick notebook vs a real script or package.

s2-g0.label: Introduction
s2-g0.desc: Machine learning is about creating computer programs that can learn from data. Instead of being explicitly programmed to perform a task, these programs improve their performance on a specific task as they are exposed to more data. This learning process allows them to make predictions or decisions without being directly told how to do so.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: An **ML engineer** sits between data science and software engineering — you build, train, deploy, and *maintain* models in production, not just prototype them in a notebook.

levels.label: vs adjacent roles
levels.body: A **data scientist** leans toward analysis and experiments; an **ML engineer** toward production systems and pipelines; an **AI engineer** often toward LLM/app integration. The overlap is large and titles vary by company.

levels.label: What you need
levels.body: Solid coding, ML fundamentals, **data pipelines**, **MLOps** (deployment, monitoring, retraining), and the communication to translate a business problem into a modelling one. In 2026, familiarity with LLMs, prompt engineering, and AI coding tools is increasingly expected alongside classical ML skills.

s3-g0.label: Data Sources
s3-g0.desc: Sources of data are origins or locations from which data is collected, categorized as primary (direct, firsthand information) or secondary (collected by others). Common primary sources include surveys, interviews, experiments, and sensor data. Secondary sources encompass databases, published reports, government data, books, articles, and web data like social media posts. Data sources can also be classified as internal (within an organization) or external (from outside sources).

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Where data comes from: **databases**, **APIs**, files, web scraping, sensors/logs, and public datasets. The source sets the ceiling on everything you build.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Distinguish **batch vs streaming**, **structured** (tables) vs **unstructured** (text/images/audio), and **labeled vs unlabeled**. Each demands different tooling.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Watch for freshness, sampling **bias**, and data contracts — *garbage in, garbage out*. Auditing the source early saves you from modelling on a flawed foundation.

s3-g1.label: Data Formats
s3-g1.desc: Data formats define the structure in which data is organized and stored. These formats dictate how information is encoded, allowing computers to interpret and process it effectively. Common examples include CSV (Comma Separated Values) for tabular data, JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) for structured data with key-value pairs, and image formats like JPEG and PNG for visual data. The choice of data format impacts storage efficiency, data accessibility, and the compatibility with different machine learning tools and algorithms.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Common containers: **CSV** (simple rows), **JSON** (nested), Excel, and binary blobs for images/audio. Pick based on structure and who consumes it.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Columnar** formats like **Parquet** are far smaller and faster for analytics than CSV; know when row-oriented vs columnar matters.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: At scale, format + compression (**Parquet/Arrow**) and **partitioning** dominate I/O cost and query speed — often more than the model itself.

s4-g0.label: Preprocessing Techniques
s4-g0.desc: Preprocessing techniques in data cleaning involve transforming raw data into a more suitable format for machine learning models. This often includes handling missing values by either imputing them with estimated values or removing rows/columns containing them. It also encompasses scaling numerical features to a similar range to prevent features with larger values from dominating the model, and encoding categorical features into numerical representations that algorithms can understand. These steps aim to improve the quality and consistency of the data, leading to better model performance.

links.title: Data Preprocessing: A Complete Guide with Python Examples
16 Data Pre Processing Techniques in 20 Minutes | Data Preprocessing in machine learning

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Raw data is messy. **Clean** it first: handle missing values, duplicates, outliers, and wrong types before any modelling.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Feature engineering** creates informative inputs; **scaling/normalisation** makes features comparable; **encoding** turns categories into numbers. Often the biggest lever on accuracy.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: **Feature selection** and **dimensionality reduction** cut noise and overfitting. Critical rule: fit every transform on the *training* split only, or you leak test information.

s5-g0.label: What is Machine Learning?
s5-g0.desc: Machine learning is a field of computer science that focuses on enabling computers to learn from data without being explicitly programmed. Instead of relying on pre-defined rules, machine learning algorithms identify patterns, make predictions, and improve their performance over time as they are exposed to more data. This learning process allows machines to adapt to new situations and solve complex problems that are difficult or impossible to address with traditional programming techniques.

links.title: Complete Machine Learning in One Video | Machine Learning Tutorial For Beginners 2025 | Simplilearn

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Programs that **learn patterns from data** instead of being hand-coded with rules. You supply examples; the algorithm infers the mapping.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Training **optimises parameters** to minimise a loss on data, but the real goal is **generalisation** — performing well on unseen examples, not memorising the training set.

levels.label: The core tension
levels.body: The **bias–variance trade-off**: too-simple models *underfit*, too-complex ones *overfit*. More data, regularisation, and honest validation keep it in check.

s5-g1.label: Types of Machine Learning
s5-g1.desc: Machine learning algorithms learn from data to make predictions or decisions. These algorithms are broadly categorized into supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning. Supervised learning uses labeled data to train a model to map inputs to outputs. Unsupervised learning, on the other hand, works with unlabeled data to discover hidden patterns and structures. Reinforcement learning involves training an agent to make decisions in an environment to maximize a reward. More recently, semi-supervised learning, which uses a combination of labeled and unlabeled data, and self-supervised learning, where the data itself provides the supervision signal, have gained prominence.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Supervised** (labeled data), **unsupervised** (structure in unlabeled data), and **reinforcement** (learn from reward) are the three pillars.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Plus **semi-supervised** (a few labels among many unlabeled) and **self-supervised** (labels derived from the data itself) — the latter powers modern LLMs and vision models.

levels.label: Why it matters
levels.body: The type you choose dictates everything downstream: data requirements, algorithms, and how you evaluate. Framing the problem correctly is half the work.

s6-g0.label: Scikit-learn
s6-g0.desc: Scikit-learn is a free and open-source Python library that provides simple and efficient tools for data analysis and machine learning. It features various algorithms for classification, regression, clustering, dimensionality reduction, model selection, and preprocessing. It's built on NumPy, SciPy, and matplotlib, making it easy to integrate with other scientific Python libraries.

links.title: Scikit-learn Crash Course - Machine Learning Library for Python

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: The standard Python library for **classical ML**, with a consistent `fit` / `predict` API across dozens of algorithms.

levels.label: The workflow
levels.body: Load → **split** (train/test) → preprocess → choose model → `fit` → **tune** → `predict` → evaluate. A `Pipeline` chains preprocessing and model into one object.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Pipelines prevent leakage (transforms fit only on train folds); `GridSearchCV` / `RandomizedSearchCV` search hyperparameters with cross-validation built in.

s7-g0.label: What is Supervised Learning?
s7-g0.desc: Supervised learning is a type of machine learning where an algorithm learns from a labeled dataset. This means that each data point in the dataset is paired with a corresponding correct output, or "label." The algorithm's goal is to learn a function that maps inputs to outputs, so that when given new, unseen inputs, it can predict the correct output based on the patterns it learned from the labeled data.

links.title: Supervised Machine Learning Explained For Beginners

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Learn from **input→output pairs**, then predict outputs for new inputs. Two flavours: **classification** (categories) and **regression** (numbers).

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: It needs **labeled data** — usually the expensive part. The model learns `f(x) ≈ y` by minimising prediction error on the labels.

levels.label: Watch out
levels.body: Label quality and **class imbalance** make or break results. Your **loss function** (cross-entropy vs MSE, etc.) defines what 'wrong' means to the model.

s7-g1.label: Classification
s7-g1.desc: Classification is a type of supervised learning where the goal is to assign data points to predefined categories or classes. Given a set of labeled data (where each data point has a known class), the algorithm learns a mapping function that can predict the class label for new, unseen data. The output is a discrete value representing the predicted class.

links.title: What is Classification in Machine Learning?
Classification in Machine Learning: A Guide for Beginners

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Predict a **category** (spam/not, digit 0–9). The output is discrete.

levels.label: Algorithms
levels.body: Logistic regression, **KNN**, **SVM**, decision trees / **random forests**, and **gradient boosting**. Most output probabilities you then threshold into a class.

levels.label: Evaluate it right
levels.body: Don't trust accuracy alone — use **precision/recall/F1** and **ROC-AUC**, especially when classes are imbalanced.

s7-g2.label: Regression
s7-g2.desc: Regression in supervised learning is a method used to predict a continuous numerical value. It works by finding the relationship between input features (independent variables) and a target variable (dependent variable). The goal is to build a model that can accurately estimate the target variable's value based on the given input features. For example, predicting house prices based on size and location is a regression problem.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Predict a **continuous number** (price, temperature). The output is a real value.

levels.label: Algorithms
levels.body: Linear and **polynomial** regression; regularised variants — **Ridge** (L2), **Lasso** (L1, also does feature selection), and **ElasticNet** — curb overfitting.

levels.label: Evaluate it right
levels.body: Use **RMSE/MAE/R²**, inspect **residuals** for leftover structure, and never trust predictions far outside the training range (extrapolation).

s8-g0.label: What is Unsupervised Learning?
s8-g0.desc: Unsupervised learning is a type of machine learning where the algorithm learns patterns from unlabeled data. Unlike supervised learning, there are no pre-defined correct answers or target variables provided to guide the learning process. Instead, the algorithm explores the data to discover hidden structures, relationships, and groupings on its own. Common tasks in unsupervised learning include clustering, dimensionality reduction, and anomaly detection.

links.title: Unsupervised Machine Learning Explained For Beginners

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **No labels.** The model finds structure on its own — grouping similar items (**clustering**) or compressing them (**dimensionality reduction**).

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Great for exploration, customer **segmentation**, **anomaly detection**, and learning representations to bootstrap other tasks.

levels.label: Watch out
levels.body: Evaluation is hard with no ground truth — lean on metrics like **silhouette score**, downstream task performance, or human review.

s8-g1.label: Clustering
s8-g1.desc: Clustering is a way to automatically group similar data points together. Imagine you have a bunch of scattered objects and you want to organize them into piles based on how alike they are. Clustering algorithms do this by finding patterns in the data, grouping data points that are close to each other in some way, and separating them from data points that are further apart. The algorithm decides which data points belong to which group, without you telling it what the groups should be beforehand.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Group **unlabeled** points so similar ones land together — customer segments, document topics, anomaly groups.

levels.label: Algorithms
levels.body: **K-means** (centroids), **hierarchical** (tree of merges), **DBSCAN** (density-based), and **Gaussian mixtures** (probabilistic). The right one depends on cluster shape and scale.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Pick `k` with the **elbow** or **silhouette** method; results swing hard on your **distance metric** and whether you scaled features first.

s8-g2.label: Dimensionality Reduction
s8-g2.desc: Dimensionality reduction is the process of reducing the number of random variables under consideration by obtaining a set of principal variables. It can be divided into feature selection and feature extraction. Feature selection selects a subset of the original features, while feature extraction transforms the data into a lower-dimensional space. The goal is to simplify the data without losing important information, making it easier to analyze and model.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Compress many features into a few while keeping the important structure — for speed, **visualisation**, and less overfitting.

levels.label: Methods
levels.body: **PCA** (linear, preserves variance), **t-SNE / UMAP** (nonlinear, for visualising), and **autoencoders** (learned compression).

levels.label: Under the hood
levels.body: PCA is an eigen/**SVD** decomposition of the covariance matrix; it fights **multicollinearity** and the **curse of dimensionality**, at some cost to interpretability.

s9-g0.label: What is Reinforcement Learning?
s9-g0.desc: Reinforcement learning is a type of machine learning where an agent learns to make decisions in an environment to maximize a reward. The agent interacts with the environment, takes actions, and receives feedback in the form of rewards or penalties. Through trial and error, the agent learns a policy that maps states to actions, aiming to accumulate the most reward over time.

links.title: Reinforcement Learning in 3 Hours | Full Course using Python

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: An **agent** acts in an **environment**, receives **rewards**, and learns a **policy** that maximises long-term reward — learning by trial and error.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Key pieces: states, actions, rewards, policy, value functions, and the **explore-vs-exploit** dilemma. Methods include **Q-learning**, **policy gradients**, and **actor-critic**.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Powerful for sequential decisions — games, robotics, and **RLHF** for aligning LLMs — but sample-hungry and often unstable to train.

s10-g0.label: What is Model Evaluation?
s10-g0.desc: Model evaluation is the process of assessing how well a machine learning model performs on a given dataset. It involves using various metrics and techniques to quantify the model's accuracy, reliability, and generalization ability. This helps determine if the model is suitable for deployment and whether further improvements are needed.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Measuring how well a model performs — crucially, on data it **didn't train on**.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Always score on a held-out **test set**; use a separate **validation set** or cross-validation for tuning so the test set stays untouched until the end.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Choose metrics that reflect the real **cost of each error type**. A single number rarely tells the whole story. Evaluation in 2026 goes beyond single metrics: use **holdout/cross-validation** for unbiased estimates, **confidence intervals** for statistical rigour, **slice-based analysis** for fairness, and **A/B testing** for real-world impact. For LLMs, evaluation is harder — human preference (arena Elo), LLM-as-judge, and task-specific benchmarks complement traditional metrics.

s10-g1.label: Why is it important?
s10-g1.desc: Model evaluation is the process of assessing how well a machine learning model performs on a given dataset. It involves using various metrics and techniques to quantify the model's accuracy, reliability, and generalization ability. This assessment helps determine if the model is suitable for deployment and provides insights into areas where it can be improved.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: A model that looks brilliant on training data can fail in the real world (**overfitting**). Evaluation catches that before your users do.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: It drives **model selection**, hyperparameter **tuning**, and the go/no-go decision to ship.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Done right, it guards against **leakage**, **distribution shift**, and vanity metrics — tying model quality to actual outcomes.

s10-g2.label: Metrics to Evaluate
s10-g2.desc: Model evaluation metrics are quantitative measures used to assess the performance of a machine learning model. These metrics provide insights into how well the model is generalizing to unseen data and help in comparing different models or tuning hyperparameters. They quantify various aspects of model behavior, such as accuracy, precision, recall, and error rate, allowing data scientists to make informed decisions about model selection and deployment.

links.title: Metrics and scoring: quantifying the quality of predictions | scikit-learn

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Classification: **accuracy, precision, recall, F1**. Regression: **RMSE, MAE, R²**.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Precision and recall trade off (read the **confusion matrix**); **ROC-AUC** summarises ranking across thresholds; **log loss** punishes confident wrong answers.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: On imbalanced data prefer **precision/recall/PR-AUC** over accuracy. Pick the metric that mirrors the cost of a false positive vs a false negative.

s10-g3.label: Validation Techniques
s10-g3.desc: Validation techniques are methods used to estimate how well a machine learning model will generalize to unseen data. They involve splitting the available data into different subsets: a training set used to train the model, and a validation set used to evaluate the model's performance during training or hyperparameter tuning. By assessing the model on data it hasn't seen before, validation techniques help to identify issues like overfitting and underfitting, and ultimately guide the selection of the best model for a given task.

links.title: The 5 Stages of Machine Learning Validation
What is the Difference Between Test and Validation Datasets?
Validating Machine Learning Model and Avoiding Common Challenges

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Split your data so you always test on **unseen** examples — typically train / validation / test.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **K-fold cross-validation** averages over k splits for a stable estimate; **stratify** to keep class ratios; **LOOCV** is the extreme one-out case.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Use **time-based splits** for temporal data (never peek into the future), and **nested CV** when you need unbiased tuning *and* evaluation.

s11-g0.label: Neural Network (NN) Basics
s11-g0.desc: A neural network is a computational model inspired by the structure and function of biological neural networks. It consists of interconnected nodes, called neurons, organized in layers. These neurons process and transmit signals, learning complex patterns from data through adjusting the strengths of the connections (weights) between them.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Layers of simple units (**neurons**) transform inputs; stacking them learns complex patterns. Each neuron computes `activation(Wx + b)`.

levels.label: How they learn
levels.body: **Forward pass** predicts → a **loss** measures error → **back-propagation** computes gradients → an **optimiser** (SGD/Adam) updates weights. Nonlinear activations (**ReLU**) give the power.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Depth + nonlinearity ≈ universal function approximation. Training success hinges on **initialisation**, **learning rate**, **normalisation**, and regularisation (**dropout**, weight decay). The **Transformer** (attention is all you need) has become the universal architecture — dominating not just NLP but vision (ViT), audio (Whisper), and multimodal tasks. **Foundation models** pretrained at scale are fine-tuned or prompted for downstream tasks, replacing task-specific architectures. State-space models (Mamba) offer an alternative for very long sequences.

s11-g1.label: Deep Learning Libraries
s11-g1.desc: Deep learning relies heavily on specialized Python libraries that provide pre-built functions and tools for building and training neural networks. TensorFlow, developed by Google, is a widely used library known for its flexibility and scalability. PyTorch, created by Facebook, is another popular choice, favored for its dynamic computation graph and ease of use, especially in research. Keras acts as a high-level API that can run on top of TensorFlow or other backends, simplifying the process of building complex models. These libraries offer functionalities like automatic differentiation, GPU acceleration, and pre-trained models, making deep learning more accessible and efficient.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Frameworks that handle **tensors**, **autodiff**, and **GPUs** so you define the model, not the gradient math.

levels.label: The big ones
levels.body: **PyTorch** (dynamic, research favourite) and **TensorFlow/Keras** (strong production tooling). Both auto-differentiate and run on GPU/TPU.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: The features that matter: **automatic differentiation**, **GPU acceleration**, efficient **data loaders**, and a path to **deployment/serving**.

s11-g2.label: Convolutional Neural Network
s11-g2.desc: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are a specialized type of artificial neural network primarily used for processing data that has a grid-like topology, such as images. They employ convolutional layers to automatically and adaptively learn spatial hierarchies of features from the input data. These layers use filters (or kernels) that slide across the input, performing element-wise multiplication and summation to detect patterns. Pooling layers are often used to reduce the spatial dimensions of the feature maps, decreasing computational complexity and making the network more robust to variations in the input.

links.title: An Introduction to Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs)
Hot Dog or Not Hot Dog – Convolutional Neural Network Course for Beginners

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Networks built for grid data like **images**. A **convolution** slides small filters across the input to detect local patterns — edges, then textures, then objects.

levels.label: Building blocks
levels.body: **Convolution**, **pooling** (downsampling), **padding**, and **strides**. Early layers learn simple features; deeper layers compose them into complex ones.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Weight sharing** + locality make CNNs parameter-efficient and translation-aware. Lineage: LeNet → AlexNet → **ResNet**; today often paired with or replaced by **vision transformers**.

s11-g3.label: Applications of CNNs
s11-g3.desc: CNNs have revolutionized the field of computer vision, leading to significant advancements in many real-world applications. Thanks to their power to solve complex problems like image classification, object detection, and facial recognition, CNNs power many applications we use daily, like automatically tagging friends in photos, enabling self-driving cars to "see" and understand their surroundings, and helping doctors diagnose diseases from medical scans. They are also used to generate realistic images, translate languages, and even create art.

links.title: Top 10 Real-World Applications of Convolutional Neural Networks

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Image **classification**, **object detection**, **segmentation**, and image/video recognition.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Also recommendation systems, medical imaging, OCR, and acting as **feature extractors** for small datasets via transfer learning.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: The default workflow is a **pretrained backbone + fine-tuning**. The same convolution idea extends to 1D (signals) and 3D (volumes).

s11-g4.label: Recurrent Neural Networks
s11-g4.desc: Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) are a type of neural network designed to process sequential data, where the order of the inputs matters. Unlike traditional feedforward networks that treat each input independently, RNNs have a "memory" that allows them to consider previous inputs when processing new ones. This memory is implemented through recurrent connections, which feed the output of a neuron back into itself or other neurons in the network, enabling the network to learn patterns and dependencies across time or sequence steps.

links.title: What is a Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)?
What is RNN? - Recurrent Neural Networks Explained

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Networks for **sequences** (text, time series) that carry a hidden **memory** from one step to the next.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Plain RNNs forget long-range context (**vanishing gradients**); **LSTM** and **GRU** add gates to remember longer.

levels.label: Today
levels.body: Largely superseded by **Transformers** for language, but still handy for streaming/low-latency settings and smaller sequence tasks.

s11-g5.label: Attention Mechanisms
s11-g5.desc: Developed by Google researchers, the attention mechanisms allow a neural network to focus on the most relevant parts of the input data when making predictions. Instead of processing the entire input uniformly, attention mechanisms assign weights to different parts of the input, indicating their importance. These weights are then used to create a weighted average of the input, which is used for further processing. This allows the model to selectively attend to the most informative parts of the input, improving performance, especially in tasks involving sequential data like text or images.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Let the model **focus on the most relevant parts** of the input for each output, instead of squashing everything into one fixed summary.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: **Self-attention** relates every token to every other token; **multi-head attention** does it in several subspaces at once. This is the heart of the **Transformer**.

levels.label: Why it changed everything
levels.body: Transformers dropped recurrence entirely — enabling parallel training and long-range context — and became the foundation of modern **LLMs**.

s12-g0.label: Autoencoders
s12-g0.desc: Autoencoders are a type of neural network used for unsupervised learning. They work by compressing the input data into a lower-dimensional representation (encoding) and then reconstructing the original input from this compressed representation (decoding). The network is trained to minimize the difference between the original input and the reconstructed output, forcing it to learn efficient and meaningful representations of the data.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Networks that **compress** data to a small code and **reconstruct** it — unsupervised representation learning.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Structure: **encoder → bottleneck → decoder**. Variants include **denoising**, **sparse**, and **variational (VAE)** autoencoders for generation.

levels.label: In practice
levels.body: Used for dimensionality reduction, **anomaly detection**, and pretraining. A **VAE** learns a latent *distribution* you can sample from to generate new data.

s12-g1.label: Generative Adversarial Networks
s12-g1.desc: Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) are a type of neural network architecture designed to generate new data that resembles the data they were trained on. They consist of two networks: a generator, which creates new data instances, and a discriminator, which evaluates the authenticity of the generated data. These two networks are trained in an adversarial process, where the generator tries to fool the discriminator, and the discriminator tries to distinguish between real and generated data. This competition drives both networks to improve, ultimately leading the generator to produce highly realistic data.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Two networks compete: a **generator** makes fakes, a **discriminator** tries to catch them — driving the generator toward realistic output.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Trained as a **minimax game**. Famous for photorealistic image generation (**StyleGAN**), super-resolution, and data augmentation.

levels.label: Reality check
levels.body: Notoriously unstable — **mode collapse**, delicate balance between the two nets. For image generation they're now largely complemented by **diffusion models**.

s12-g2.label: Explainable AI
s12-g2.desc: Explainable AI (XAI) focuses on making machine learning models and their decisions understandable to humans. Instead of treating models as black boxes, XAI aims to provide insights into how a model arrives at a particular prediction or decision. This involves developing techniques that allow us to interpret the model's internal logic, identify the factors that influence its outputs, and assess its reliability and potential biases.

links.title: Explainable AI: Demystifying AI Agents Decision-Making

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Techniques to understand **why** a model predicted what it did — essential for trust, debugging, and regulation.

levels.label: Methods
levels.body: **Feature importance**, **SHAP**, **LIME**, and attention/saliency maps. Explanations can be **global** (whole model) or **local** (one prediction).

levels.label: Why it matters
levels.body: Central to **fairness**, accountability, and high-stakes domains (health, finance). There's often a real **interpretability vs accuracy** trade-off.

s12-g3.label: Natural Language Processing
s12-g3.desc: Natural Language Processing (NLP) is a field focused on enabling computers to understand, interpret, and generate human language. It bridges the gap between human communication and computer understanding by developing algorithms and models that can process and analyze text and speech data. This allows machines to perform tasks like translation, sentiment analysis, and text summarization.

links.title: What is Natural Language Processing (NLP)? | AWS
Stanford’s Natural Language Processing with Deep Learning

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Teaching machines to work with human language — classify, translate, summarise, answer, and generate text.

levels.label: Pipeline pieces
levels.body: **Tokenisation**, **stemming/lemmatisation**, and **embeddings** (words → vectors). **Attention models** capture context between words.

levels.label: Modern NLP
levels.body: It's now **Transformer-based**: pretrain on huge text corpora (self-supervised), then fine-tune or prompt — the recipe behind today's **LLMs**.

s0-g0-l0.label: Derivatives, Partial Derivatives
s0-g0-l0.desc: A derivative measures how a function changes as its input changes. Imagine a curve on a graph; the derivative at a specific point tells you the slope of the line tangent to that curve at that point. When dealing with functions of multiple variables, we use partial derivatives. A partial derivative measures how a function changes with respect to one specific variable, while holding all other variables constant.

links.title: What is a Derivative? Deriving the Power Rule

s0-g0-l1.label: Chain rule of derivation
s0-g0-l1.desc: The chain rule is a formula for finding the derivative of a composite function. If you have a function that's made up of one function inside another (like sin(x²) ), the chain rule lets you break down the differentiation process. It states that the derivative of the composite function is the derivative of the outer function evaluated at the inner function, multiplied by the derivative of the inner function.

links.title: Derivatives of Composite Functions: The Chain Rule

s0-g0-l2.label: Gradient, Jacobian, Hessian
s0-g0-l2.desc: The gradient, Jacobian, and Hessian are fundamental tools from calculus used to analyze and optimize functions, especially in the context of machine learning. The gradient of a scalar-valued function of multiple variables is a vector containing the partial derivatives with respect to each variable, indicating the direction of the steepest ascent. The Jacobian matrix generalizes the gradient to vector-valued functions of multiple variables, containing all the partial derivatives of each output component with respect to each input variable. The Hessian matrix, on the other hand, is the square matrix of second-order partial derivatives of a scalar-valued function, providing information about the local curvature of the function.

links.title: A Gentle Introduction to the Jacobian
A Gentle Introduction To Hessian Matrices
Partial Derivatives and the Gradient of a Function
Change of Variables and the Jacobian
Multivariable Calculus: Lecture 3 Hessian Matrix : Optimization for a three variable function

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: The **gradient** ∇f stacks first derivatives and points uphill; the **Jacobian** generalises it to vector-valued functions; the **Hessian** holds second derivatives and reveals curvature.

s0-g1-l0.label: Matrix & Matrix Operations
s0-g1-l0.desc: A matrix is a rectangular array of numbers, symbols, or expressions, arranged in rows and columns. Matrix operations are the rules and procedures for manipulating these matrices. These operations include addition, subtraction, multiplication, transposition (flipping rows and columns), and finding the inverse of a matrix, each with specific rules about the dimensions of the matrices involved.

s0-g1-l1.label: Scalars, Vectors, Tensors
s0-g1-l1.desc: Scalars, vectors, and tensors are fundamental building blocks for representing data in machine learning. A scalar is a single numerical value, like a temperature reading. A vector is an ordered array of numbers, representing a point in space or a feature set for a single data instance. A tensor is a generalization of vectors and matrices to higher dimensions; it can be thought of as a multi-dimensional array, useful for representing images, videos, or complex datasets with multiple features and relationships.

links.title: From Vectors to Tensors: Exploring the Mathematics of Tensor Algebra
What the HECK is a Tensor?!?

s0-g1-l2.label: Singular Value Decomposition
s0-g1-l2.desc: Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) is a matrix factorization technique that decomposes a rectangular matrix into three other matrices: a unitary matrix, a diagonal matrix of singular values, and another unitary matrix. This decomposition reveals the underlying structure of the original matrix, highlighting its principal components and allowing for dimensionality reduction and noise removal. Essentially, SVD breaks down a complex matrix into simpler, more manageable components that capture the most important information.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Any matrix factors as `A = UΣVᵀ` — rotate, scale, rotate. The singular values in **Σ** rank directions by importance; keeping the top-k gives the best low-rank approximation.

s0-g1-l3.label: Determinants, inverse of Matrix
s0-g1-l3.desc: A determinant is a scalar value that can be computed from the elements of a square matrix and encodes certain properties of the linear transformation described by the matrix. The inverse of a matrix, denoted as A⁻¹, is another matrix that, when multiplied by the original matrix A, results in the identity matrix. The inverse exists only for square matrices with a non-zero determinant, making the matrix invertible.

s0-g1-l4.label: Eigenvalues, Diagonalization
s0-g1-l4.desc: Eigenvalues are special numbers associated with a square matrix that, when multiplied by a corresponding eigenvector, result in the same vector scaled by that eigenvalue. Diagonalization is the process of transforming a square matrix into a diagonal matrix, where all off-diagonal elements are zero, using its eigenvectors and eigenvalues. This transformation simplifies many matrix operations and provides insights into the matrix's properties.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: An **eigenvector** keeps its direction under a matrix: `Av = λv`, with eigenvalue **λ** the scaling factor. Diagonalisation `A = QΛQ⁻¹` makes matrix powers trivial.

s0-g3-l0.label: Basics of Probability
s0-g3-l0.desc: Probability is a way to quantify the likelihood of an event occurring. It provides a numerical measure, ranging from 0 to 1, representing the chance that a specific outcome will happen. A probability of 0 indicates impossibility, while a probability of 1 signifies certainty. Understanding probability involves concepts like random variables, probability distributions, and events, which are essential for making predictions and decisions under uncertainty.

links.title: Probability and Statistics: The Science of Uncertainty

s0-g3-l1.label: Bayes Theorem
s0-g3-l1.desc: Bayes' Theorem is a mathematical formula that describes how to update the probability of a hypothesis based on new evidence. It essentially calculates the probability of an event occurring given that another event has already occurred. The theorem uses prior knowledge of conditions related to the event to refine the probability estimate as new information becomes available.

links.title: Bayes' Theorem: What It Is, Formula, and Examples

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: `P(A|B) = P(B|A)·P(A) / P(B)`. It flips a conditional — turning *likelihood* into *posterior* — and underpins Naive Bayes and all Bayesian inference.

s0-g3-l2.label: Random Variances, PDFs
s0-g3-l2.desc:
A random variable is a variable whose value is a numerical outcome of a random phenomenon. It can be discrete (taking on a finite or countably infinite number of values) or continuous (taking on any value within a given range).

The probability density function (PDF) describes the relative likelihood for a continuous random variable to take on a given value. It's important to note that the value of the PDF at any given point is not a probability itself, but rather the area under the PDF curve over a given interval represents the probability of the random variable falling within that interval.

links.title: Random Variable: What is it in Statistics?
The Basics of Probability Density Function (PDF), With an Example
Sample Variance in Random Population Sampling

s0-g3-l3.label: Types of Distribution
s0-g3-l3.desc: A distribution describes how data is spread or arranged across its possible values. It provides a way to understand the probability of different outcomes occurring within a dataset. Different types of distributions, like normal, uniform, or binomial, each have unique characteristics that determine the likelihood of observing specific values. Understanding these distributions is essential for summarizing data, making predictions, and performing statistical inference.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Know the **Normal** (bell curve, CLT), **Bernoulli/Binomial** (yes-no trials), **Poisson** (rare counts), and **Uniform**. Picking the right one is half of probabilistic modelling.

s0-g3-l4.label: Descriptive Statistics
s0-g3-l4.desc: Descriptive statistics involves methods for summarizing and organizing data in a meaningful way. It focuses on describing the main features of a dataset using measures like mean, median, mode, standard deviation, and range. These techniques help to understand the central tendency, variability, and distribution of the data without making inferences beyond the specific dataset.

links.title: Descriptive Statistics: Definition, Overview, Types, and Examples

s0-g3-l5.label: Inferential Statistics
s0-g3-l5.desc: Inferential statistics uses sample data to make inferences or predictions about a larger population. Instead of examining the entire population, which is often impractical or impossible, we analyze a representative subset (the sample) and then use statistical methods to draw conclusions about the characteristics of the whole population. This involves estimating population parameters (like the mean or proportion) and testing hypotheses about these parameters based on the sample data.

links.title: Inferential Statistics | An Easy Introduction & Examples

s0-g3-l6.label: Basic concepts
s0-g3-l6.desc:
Statistics is the science of collecting, analyzing, interpreting, and presenting data. It provides the foundation for understanding patterns and making inferences from data, which is crucial for machine learning algorithms. Here are 10 basic statistical concepts:

*   **Mean:** The average value of a dataset, calculated by summing all values and dividing by the number of values.
*   **Median:** The middle value in a sorted dataset.
*   **Mode:** The value that appears most frequently in a dataset.
*   **Standard Deviation:** A measure of the spread or dispersion of data points around the mean.
*   **Variance:** The square of the standard deviation, representing the average squared difference from the mean.
*   **Probability:** The likelihood of an event occurring, expressed as a number between 0 and 1.
*   **Distributions:** A function that shows the possible values for a variable and how often they occur (e.g., normal distribution, uniform distribution).
*   **Hypothesis Testing:** A method for testing a claim or hypothesis about a population based on a sample of data.
*   **Correlation:** A statistical measure that describes the extent to which two variables are related.
*   **Regression:** A statistical method for modeling the relationship between a dependent variable and one or more independent variables.

s0-g3-l7.label: Graphs & Charts
s0-g3-l7.desc: Graphs and charts are visual representations of data. They use symbols like bars, lines, and slices to display patterns, trends, and relationships within datasets. These visual tools help in understanding complex information quickly and making data more accessible and interpretable.

s1-g1-l0.label: Variables and Data Types
s1-g1-l0.desc: Variables are named storage locations in a computer's memory used to hold data. Data types classify the kind of value a variable can hold, such as numbers (integers, decimals), text (strings), or boolean values (true/false). Understanding variables and data types is fundamental to writing any program, as it dictates how data is stored, manipulated, and used within the code.

s1-g1-l1.label: Data Structures
s1-g1-l1.desc: Python provides several built-in data structures for organizing and storing data. These structures include lists, which are ordered and mutable collections; tuples, which are ordered and immutable collections; dictionaries, which store data in key-value pairs; and sets, which are unordered collections of unique elements. Each data structure offers different performance characteristics and is suitable for various tasks depending on the specific requirements of the program.

links.title: Data Structures and Algorithms in Python - Full Course for Beginners

s1-g1-l2.label: Loops
s1-g1-l2.desc: Loops in Python are a way to repeat a block of code multiple times. They allow you to execute a set of instructions over and over, either a specific number of times or until a certain condition is met. Python has two main types of loops: `for` loops, which are typically used to iterate over a sequence (like a list or string), and `while` loops, which continue executing as long as a given condition remains true.

links.title: Python While Loops & For Loops | Python tutorial for Beginners

s1-g1-l3.label: Conditionals
s1-g1-l3.desc: Conditional statements in Python allow you to execute different blocks of code based on whether a certain condition is true or false. The most common conditional statements are `if`, `elif` (else if), and `else`. An `if` statement checks a condition, and if it's true, the code block under it runs. `elif` allows you to check additional conditions if the initial `if` condition is false. Finally, `else` provides a block of code to execute if none of the preceding `if` or `elif` conditions are true. These statements enable programs to make decisions and respond differently to various inputs or situations.

links.title: Learn Python CONDITIONAL EXPRESSIONS in 5 minutes!

s1-g1-l4.label: Exceptions
s1-g1-l4.desc: Exceptions in Python are events that disrupt the normal flow of a program's execution. They occur when the interpreter encounters an error during runtime, such as trying to divide by zero or accessing an index that's out of bounds in a list. When an exception occurs, Python creates an exception object. If the exception isn't handled, the program will terminate and display an error message. However, you can use `try` and `except` blocks to catch and handle exceptions, allowing your program to continue running even when errors occur.

links.title: Learn Python EXCEPTION HANDLING in 5 minutes!

s1-g1-l5.label: Functions, Builtin Functions
s1-g1-l5.desc: Functions are reusable blocks of code that perform a specific task. They take inputs, process them, and return an output. Python provides many built-in functions like `print()` for displaying output, `len()` for finding the length of a sequence, and `type()` for determining the data type of a variable. These built-in functions are readily available for use, while you can also define your own custom functions, also known as user-defined functions (UDFs), to encapsulate specific logic and improve code organization.

links.title: Python Functions: How to Call & Write Functions
Functions in Python | Python for Beginners

s1-g3-l0.label: Numpy
s1-g3-l0.desc: NumPy is a fundamental Python library used for numerical computing. It provides support for large, multi-dimensional arrays and matrices, along with a collection of mathematical functions to operate on these arrays efficiently. These arrays are homogeneous, meaning they contain elements of the same data type, which allows for optimized storage and computation. NumPy is widely used in data science, machine learning, and scientific computing due to its performance and ease of use.

s1-g3-l1.label: Pandas
s1-g3-l1.desc: Pandas is a library written for the Python programming language for data manipulation and analysis. In particular, it offers data structures and operations for manipulating numerical tables and time series.

links.title: Complete Python Pandas Data Science Tutorial! (2025 Updated Edition)

s1-g3-l2.label: Matplotlib
s1-g3-l2.desc: Matplotlib is a paramount data visualization library used extensively by data analysts for generating a wide array of plots and graphs. Through Matplotlib, data analysts can convey results clearly and effectively, driving insights from complex data sets. It offers a hierarchical environment which is very natural for a data scientist to work with. Providing an object-oriented API, it allows for extensive customization and integration into larger applications. From histograms, bar charts, scatter plots to 3D graphs, the versatility of Matplotlib assists data analysts in the better comprehension and compelling representation of data.

s1-g3-l3.label: Seaborn
s1-g3-l3.desc: Seaborn is a robust, comprehensive Python library focused on the creation of informative and attractive statistical graphics. As a data analyst, seaborn plays an essential role in elaborating complex visual stories with the data. It aids in understanding the data by providing an interface for drawing attractive and informative statistical graphics. Seaborn is built on top of Python's core visualization library Matplotlib, and is integrated with data structures from Pandas. This makes seaborn an integral tool for data visualization in the data analyst's toolkit, making the exploration and understanding of data easier and more intuitive.

s1-g4-l0.label: Databases (SQL, No-SQL)
s1-g4-l0.desc: Databases are organized collections of data, stored and accessed electronically. SQL databases, like MySQL or PostgreSQL, use a structured, table-based format with a predefined schema, enforcing relationships between data through keys. NoSQL databases, such as MongoDB or Cassandra, offer more flexible data models like document, key-value, or graph, allowing for unstructured or semi-structured data and often prioritizing scalability and speed over strict consistency.

links.title: Types of Databases: Relational, NoSQL, Cloud, Vector

s1-g4-l1.label: Internet
s1-g4-l1.desc: The internet serves as a vast repository of information that can be leveraged for machine learning projects. This includes publicly available datasets, web pages that can be scraped for relevant information, social media platforms where user-generated content provides insights into opinions and trends, and online databases that offer structured data on various topics. These sources provide a diverse range of data types, from text and images to numerical and categorical data, enabling the development of a wide array of machine learning models.

s1-g4-l2.label: APIs
s1-g4-l2.desc: Application Programming Interfaces, better known as APIs, play a fundamental role in the work of data analysts, particularly in the process of data collection. APIs are sets of protocols, routines, and tools that enable different software applications to communicate with each other. In data analysis, APIs are used extensively to collect, exchange, and manipulate data from different sources in a secure and efficient manner. This data collection process is paramount in shaping the insights derived by the analysts.

s1-g4-l3.label: Mobile Apps
s1-g4-l3.desc: Mobile app data refers to the information generated and collected from applications running on mobile devices like smartphones and tablets. This data encompasses a wide range of user interactions, device characteristics, and app performance metrics. It can include user demographics, in-app behavior, location data, device type, operating system, and network information, among other things.

s1-g4-l4.label: IoT
s1-g4-l4.desc:
The Internet of Things (IoT) refers to the network of physical devices, vehicles, home appliances, and other items embedded with electronics, software, sensors, and actuators that enable these objects to connect and exchange data. These devices continuously generate vast amounts of data reflecting their status, environment, and interactions. This data can include sensor readings like temperature, pressure, humidity, location, and images or video streams.

*   [@article@What is the Internet of Things (IoT)?](https://www.ibm.com/think/topics/internet-of-things)
*   [@article@Internet of Things](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Internet_of_things)
*   [@video@What is IoT (Internet of Things)? An Introduction](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4FxU-xpuCww)

links.title: What is the Internet of Things (IoT)?
What is IoT (Internet of Things)? An Introduction
What is the Internet of Things (IoT)?

s2-g0-l0.label: What is an ML Engineer?
s2-g0-l0.desc: An ML Engineer focuses on building, deploying, and maintaining machine learning systems in production. They bridge the gap between data science and software engineering, taking models developed by data scientists and making them scalable, reliable, and efficient for real-world applications. This involves tasks like data pipeline construction, model deployment, performance monitoring, and infrastructure management.

links.title: What Is a Machine Learning Engineer? (+ How to Get Started)

s2-g0-l1.label: ML Engineer vs AI Engineer
s2-g0-l1.desc: An ML Engineer primarily concentrates on building, deploying, and maintaining machine learning models in production environments. An AI Engineer, on the other hand, typically has a broader scope, encompassing the design and development of entire AI systems, which may include components beyond just machine learning, such as natural language processing, computer vision, and robotics.

links.title: AI VS ML Engineer What Do They Do?

s2-g0-l2.label: Skills and Responsibilities
s2-g0-l2.desc: Machine learning roles require a blend of technical expertise and practical abilities. These roles involve designing, developing, and deploying machine learning models to solve real-world problems. Key skills include proficiency in programming languages like Python, a strong understanding of statistical concepts, and experience with machine learning frameworks. Responsibilities often encompass data collection and preprocessing, model selection and training, performance evaluation, and continuous model improvement.

s3-g1-l0.label: CSV
s3-g1-l0.desc: CSV or Comma Separated Values files play an integral role in data collection for data analysts. These file types allow the efficient storage of data and are commonly generated by spreadsheet software like Microsoft Excel or Google Sheets, but their simplicity makes them compatible with a variety of applications that deal with data. In the context of data analysis, CSV files are extensively used to import and export large datasets, making them essential for any data analyst's toolkit. They allow analysts to organize vast amounts of information into a structured format, which is fundamental in extracting useful insights from raw data.

links.title: What is a CSV file: A comprehensive guide

s3-g1-l1.label: Excel
s3-g1-l1.desc: Excel files are a common way to store data in a structured format using rows and columns. Each cell in the spreadsheet can hold different types of data, like numbers, text, or dates. These files are often used for organizing, analyzing, and visualizing data because they are easy to create and manipulate using spreadsheet software.

s3-g1-l2.label: JSON
s3-g1-l2.desc: JavaScript Object Notation (JSON) is a standard text-based format for representing structured data based on JavaScript object syntax. It is commonly used for transmitting data in web applications (e.g., sending some data from the server to the client, so it can be displayed on a web page, or vice versa).

s3-g1-l3.label: Parquet
s3-g1-l3.desc: Parquet is a columnar storage format designed for efficient data storage and retrieval. Unlike row-oriented formats, Parquet stores data by columns, which allows for better compression and faster query performance when only a subset of columns are needed. This makes it particularly well-suited for big data processing and analytics, where large datasets are common and queries often target specific columns.

s3-g1-l4.label: Other Data Formats
s3-g1-l4.desc: Beyond the common formats like CSV, Excel, JSON, and Parquet, data can exist in a variety of other structures. These include formats optimized for specific applications or data types. For instance, images are often stored as JPEGs or PNGs, while audio data might be in WAV or MP3 format. Relational databases store data in structured tables accessible through SQL. Furthermore, specialized formats like HDF5 are used for large, complex datasets, particularly in scientific computing, and Protocol Buffers offer an efficient way to serialize structured data. Data can also be unstructured, existing as plain text, log files, or even streaming data from sensors.

s4-g0-l0.label: Data Cleaning
s4-g0-l0.desc: Data cleaning, which is often referred as data cleansing or data scrubbing, is one of the most important and initial steps in the data analysis process. As a data analyst, the bulk of your work often revolves around understanding, cleaning, and standardizing raw data before analysis. Data cleaning involves identifying, correcting or removing any errors or inconsistencies in datasets in order to improve their quality. The process is crucial because it directly determines the accuracy of the insights you generate - garbage in, garbage out. Even the most sophisticated models and visualizations would not be of much use if they're based on dirty data. Therefore, mastering data cleaning techniques is essential for any data analyst.

s4-g0-l1.label: Feature Engineering
s4-g0-l1.desc: Feature engineering is the process of transforming raw data into features that better represent the underlying problem to the predictive models, resulting in improved model accuracy. This involves selecting, transforming, and creating new features from existing data. The goal is to extract more information from the data and make it easier for machine learning algorithms to learn patterns and make accurate predictions.

links.title: What is a feature engineering? | IBM
Feature Engineering in Machine Learning: A Practical Guide
What is feature engineering | Feature Engineering Tutorial Python

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Turning raw data into informative inputs — ratios, date parts, aggregations, interactions. Often **more impactful than the model choice** itself.

s4-g0-l2.label: Feature Scaling & Normalization
s4-g0-l2.desc: Feature scaling is a preprocessing technique in machine learning that transforms numerical features to a common scale, ensuring they contribute equally to the model by preventing features with larger ranges from dominating, which is particularly important for algorithms sensitive to feature scales like gradient descent-based methods. Key methods include Standardization (transforming data to a mean of 0 and a standard deviation of 1, often better for outliers), and Normalization (scaling data to a fixed range, often 0 to 1).

links.title: Normalization vs. Standardization: How to Know the Difference

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **Standardisation** rescales to mean 0, std 1 (`(x−μ)/σ`); **min-max** squashes to [0,1]. Vital for distance- and gradient-based models (KNN, SVM, neural nets); trees don't care.

s4-g0-l3.label: Dimensionality Reduction
s4-g0-l3.desc: Dimensionality reduction is the process of reducing the number of random variables under consideration by obtaining a set of principal variables. It can be divided into feature selection and feature extraction. Feature selection selects a subset of the original features, while feature extraction transforms the data into a lower-dimensional space. The goal is to simplify the data without losing important information, making it easier to analyze and model.

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: Fight the **curse of dimensionality**: collapse correlated features with **PCA** (linear) or **t-SNE/UMAP** (non-linear, for visualising). Fewer dimensions, less overfitting.

s4-g0-l4.label: Feature Selection
s4-g0-l4.desc: Feature selection is the process of choosing a subset of the most relevant features from your original dataset. The goal is to reduce the dimensionality of the data by removing irrelevant, redundant, or noisy features. This can lead to simpler models, improved model performance (e.g., accuracy, speed), and better understanding of the underlying data.

s5-g1-l0.label: Supervised Learning
s5-g1-l0.desc: Supervised learning is a type of machine learning where an algorithm learns from a labeled dataset. This means that each data point in the dataset is paired with a corresponding correct output, or "label." The algorithm's goal is to learn a function that maps inputs to outputs, so that when given new, unseen inputs, it can predict the correct output based on the patterns it learned from the labeled data.

links.title: Supervised Machine Learning Explained For Beginners

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: Learns from **labelled** examples (input → target). Splits into **classification** (discrete labels) and **regression** (continuous values).

s5-g1-l1.label: Unsupervised Learning
s5-g1-l1.desc: Unsupervised learning is a type of machine learning where the algorithm learns patterns from unlabeled data. Unlike supervised learning, there are no pre-defined correct answers or target variables provided to guide the learning process. Instead, the algorithm explores the data to discover hidden structures, relationships, and groupings on its own. Common tasks in unsupervised learning include clustering, dimensionality reduction, and anomaly detection.

links.title: Unsupervised Machine Learning Explained For Beginners

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: **No labels** — the model finds structure itself: **clustering** (groups), **dimensionality reduction**, density estimation.

s5-g1-l2.label: Semi-supervised Learning
s5-g1-l2.desc: Semi-supervised learning is a type of machine learning where the training data contains both labeled and unlabeled examples. The goal is to leverage the information from the unlabeled data to improve the performance of a model that would otherwise be trained solely on the labeled data. This approach is particularly useful when obtaining labels is expensive or time-consuming, but unlabeled data is readily available.

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: A **few labels plus lots of unlabelled** data. Cheaper than full labelling; the unlabelled mass sharpens the decision boundary.

s5-g1-l3.label: Self-supervised Learning
s5-g1-l3.desc: Self-supervised learning is a type of machine learning where the model learns from unlabeled data by creating its own supervisory signals. This is achieved by masking parts of the input data and training the model to predict the masked portions based on the remaining data. In essence, the data itself provides the labels, allowing the model to learn useful representations without requiring explicit human annotation.

links.title: Mark Zuckerberg: AI Learns More Efficiently With Self-Supervised Learning

levels.label: Going deeper
levels.body: The data **labels itself** — predict a masked word or the next token. This is how modern LLMs and foundation models are pretrained at scale.

s5-g1-l4.label: Reinforcement Learning
s5-g1-l4.desc: Reinforcement learning is a type of machine learning where an agent learns to make decisions in an environment to maximize a reward. The agent interacts with the environment, takes actions, and receives feedback in the form of rewards or penalties. Through trial and error, the agent learns a policy that maps states to actions, aiming to accumulate the most reward over time.

links.title: Reinforcement Learning in 3 Hours | Full Course using Python

levels.label: Basics
levels.body: An **agent** acts in an environment to maximise cumulative **reward**. No fixed dataset — it learns from trial, error, and delayed feedback.

s6-g0-l0.label: Data Loading
s6-g0-l0.desc: Data loading in Scikit-learn refers to the process of importing datasets into a format that can be used for machine learning tasks. This involves reading data from various sources, such as CSV files, databases, or even directly from NumPy arrays, and structuring it into a format that Scikit-learn's algorithms can understand, typically NumPy arrays or Pandas DataFrames. The loaded data is then usually split into features (independent variables) and a target variable (dependent variable) for training and evaluating machine learning models.

links.title: Scikit-Learn Full Crash Course - Python Machine Learning

s6-g0-l1.label: Train - Test Data
s6-g0-l1.desc: When building a machine learning model, we usually split our dataset into two parts: a training set and a testing set. The training set is used to teach the model how to make predictions, while the testing set is used to evaluate how well the model has learned. This helps us understand if the model can generalize to new, unseen data. In scikit-learn, you can easily split your data using the `train_test_split` function from the `model_selection` module. You provide your data and labels to this function, and it returns the split datasets. You can also specify the proportion of data to be used for testing.

links.title: Split Your Dataset With scikit-learn's train_test_split()
Train Test Split with Python Machine Learning (Scikit-Learn)

s6-g0-l2.label: Data Preparation
s6-g0-l2.desc: Scikit-learn provides tools to get your data ready for machine learning models. This often involves cleaning, transforming, and scaling your data. Cleaning might mean handling missing values using techniques like imputation. Transformation can involve converting categorical features into numerical ones using methods like one-hot encoding. Scaling ensures that all features contribute equally to the model by bringing them to a similar range, using techniques like standardization or normalization. These steps help improve the performance and accuracy of your machine learning models.

links.title: Scikit-learn Crash Course - Machine Learning Library for Python

s6-g0-l3.label: Model Selection
s6-g0-l3.desc: Model selection is the process of choosing the best machine learning model from a set of candidate models for a given task. Scikit-learn offers a wide range of models, including linear models (like linear regression and logistic regression), tree-based models (like decision trees and random forests), support vector machines (SVMs), and neural networks, enabling you to find the most suitable model for your specific problem.

s6-g0-l4.label: Tuning
s6-g0-l4.desc: Scikit-learn provides tools to find the best settings (hyperparameters) for your machine learning models. Instead of manually trying different values, you can use techniques like GridSearchCV or RandomizedSearchCV. These methods systematically test a range of hyperparameter combinations using cross-validation to evaluate performance. The goal is to identify the hyperparameter set that yields the best model performance on your data, improving accuracy and generalization.

links.title: Tuning the hyper-parameters of an estimator
A Comprehensive Guide to Cross-Validation with Scikit-Learn and Python
Hands-On Hyperparameter Tuning with Scikit-Learn: Tips and Tricks

s6-g0-l5.label: Prediction
s6-g0-l5.desc: In scikit-learn, prediction means using a trained machine learning model to estimate an output (or target variable) for new, unseen data. After a model is trained on a dataset, it learns the relationship between the input features and the target variable. To make a prediction, you provide the model with new input features, and it uses the learned relationship to generate a predicted value for the target. This is typically done using the `.predict()` method on a trained model object, which takes the new data as input and returns the model's predictions.

links.title: Metrics and scoring: quantifying the quality of predictions
How to Make Predictions with scikit-learn

s7-g1-l0.label: K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN)
s7-g1-l0.desc: K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) is a simple algorithm used for classifying data points based on their proximity to other data points. Given a new, unclassified data point, KNN identifies the 'K' closest data points (neighbors) from the training dataset. The class that appears most frequently among these 'K' neighbors is then assigned as the class of the new data point.
